content:
2 min read
Just now

--

Sharing my work didn’t just help others follow my journey. It helped me understand my own journey better.
When I started learning Software Engineering, I believed I should only share finished work.
Complete projects.
Polished code.
Perfect architectures.
I thought people were interested only in results.
So I kept most of my learning to myself.
Until one day, I decided to do the opposite.
Instead of waiting until everything was “ready,” I started sharing the process.
Looking back, it became one of the best decisions I’ve made as a learner.
Perfection Was Holding Me Back
At first, I thought I needed to know more before publishing anything.
I wanted my projects to be cleaner.
My architecture to be better.
My code to look more professional.
But the more I learned, the more I realized something.
There is no finish line.
Software is always evolving.
And so are we.
Waiting for perfection often means never sharing at all.
Writing Forces Me to Think
One unexpected benefit of building in public was how much writing improved my understanding.
Every time I write an article or explain a concept, I have to organize my thoughts.
I have to connect ideas.
I have to justify decisions.
And sometimes, I discover that I don’t understand a topic as well as I thought.
Writing doesn’t just communicate knowledge.
It exposes the gaps in it.
Feedback Became Part of My Learning
Another thing surprised me.
The moment I started sharing my work, I stopped learning alone.
People asked questions I hadn’t considered.
They suggested different approaches.
They challenged some of my assumptions.
Not every piece of feedback was something I adopted.
But every conversation made me think more carefully about my decisions.
Learning became collaborative.
My GitHub Became More Than a Repository
I used to see GitHub as a place to store code.
Today, I see it differently.
Every repository tells a story.
Every README explains a decision.
Every commit captures a small step in my learning journey.
Instead of hiding unfinished work, I started documenting the evolution of my projects.
That mindset completely changed the way I build software.
Building in Public Changed My Standards
Knowing that someone might read my code changed how I write it.
I started choosing better names.
Writing clearer documentation.
Organizing projects more carefully.
Thinking about maintainability from the beginning.
Not because someone demanded it.
But because explaining my work made me more aware of its quality.
It’s Not About Followers
One misconception I had was believing that building in public was mainly about growing an audience.
Today, I don’t think that’s the biggest benefit.
The greatest value isn’t visibility.
It’s accountability.
Sharing my progress encourages me to keep learning.
To keep building.
To keep improving.
Even when no one is watching.
Progress Is More Valuable Than Perfection
Looking back, I’m glad I didn’t wait until I felt “ready.”
If I had, I probably wouldn’t have written many of the articles that helped organize my own thinking.
Building in public isn’t about proving expertise.
It’s about documenting growth.
And growth is rarely perfect.
